questions.question: Define message queues and FIFOs (named pipes) as IPC mechanisms in Unix/Linux. Differentiate between them with respect to direction of communication and data persistence.
keyPoints: Message queues: kernel-persistent message passing mechanism with message types
FIFOs: named pipes that appear as special files in filesystem
FIFOs are unidirectional; message queues support bidirectional communication
Both persist until explicitly removed; message queues use msgget/ msgsnd/ msgrcv system calls

questions.question: Explain signals as an interprocess communication mechanism in operating systems. Differentiate between synchronous and asynchronous signals with suitable examples.
keyPoints: Signals are software interrupts for event notification between processes
Synchronous signals occur due to process actions (e.g., SIGSEGV, SIGFPE); asynchronous signals external (e.g., SIGINT, SIGALRM)
Signal handling using signal() or sigaction() system calls
Limited data transfer; primarily used for process control and notification

questions.question: Discuss the various IPC mechanisms in Unix/Linux operating systems. Compare pipes, message queues, semaphores, and shared memory in terms of data transfer mode, synchronization requirements, and performance characteristics.
keyPoints: Pipes: byte-stream communication, parent-child relationship required, unidirectional
Message queues: structured message passing with message types, kernel-managed queues
Semaphores: counting variables for process synchronization, not data transfer
Shared memory: fastest IPC, direct memory access, requires explicit synchronization (mutex/semaphores)

questions.question: Explain sockets as an IPC mechanism. Distinguish between stream sockets (SOCK_STREAM) and datagram sockets (SOCK_DGRAM). Discuss their application in both local interprocess communication and network communication.
keyPoints: Sockets: endpoint for communication, bidirectional, support both local and network communication
Stream sockets: connection-oriented, reliable, ordered, TCP protocol
Datagram sockets: connectionless, unreliable, no ordering, UDP protocol
Unix domain sockets (AF_UNIX) for local IPC; Internet sockets (AF_INET) for network communication
